title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
How To Calculate The Cash Surrender Value Of Life Insurance
Keywords: cash surrender value, calculate surrender value, surrender value
Meta Description: Surrendering your life insurance plan terminates your coverage immediately. If you’re planning on surrendering your policy, knowing how to calculate its surrender value is important. Read on to find out more about cash surrender values and alternatives to surrendering your life insurance policy.
A life insurance policy can help you secure the financial futures of your family members, even if anything unfortunate were to happen to you. However, there may be certain situations where you may need to surrender the policy before it matures. This is where knowing about the surrender value of your life insurance plan becomes essential.
Surrender Value and Associated Charges
Whether you’re tired of paying high premiums or you no longer have beneficiaries who need the death benefit endowments from your plan, you need to know the cash surrender value of your life insurance policy before terminating it.
Cash surrender value refers to the amount you will receive from your insurer when you cancel a universal or whole life plan before it matures. In other words, if you terminate your policy mid-term, you will be eligible for a certain payout amount against the premiums you’ve paid. Additionally, as per the latest IRDAI1 directives, if you choose to terminate the policy after 5 years, no surrender charges can be imposed on it by your insurance provider.
If you’re wondering how this surrender value is calculated, fret not, for we’ve got you covered! In this article, we review the process of calculating the surrender value of a life insurance plan as well as the alternatives to surrendering your policy.
How is Cash Surrender Value Calculated?
Calculating the surrender value of your insurance plan is quite simple. All you need to do is add the total premiums paid and subtract the charges levied by your insurer for surrendering the plan mid-term.
However, this is not a blanket calculation process. Before you can calculate the surrender value of your life insurance policy, you must know the difference between guaranteed surrender value and special surrender value as the cash surrender value of your policy will be hinged on these two key concepts.
Types of Surrender Values and How to Calculate Them
Guaranteed Surrender Value: The thumb rule for guaranteed surrender value is that it keeps increasing with time. You can claim this surrender value after 3 years of paying your insurance premiums. Usually, this amounts to 30% of the premiums you’ve paid, excluding the first-year payments.
For instance, say you have invested in a life insurance policy with a sum assured of 3 lakhs. In the first 3 years you’ve paid ₹ 45,000. The cash surrender value of your policy after the first 3 years will be ₹ 9000 – that’s 30% of ₹ 30,000 (excluding the first-year payments).
Special Surrender Value: The special surrender value of your policy is based on something known as the paid-up value. If you stop paying the premiums for your life insurance policy, the plan continues but with a lower sum assured. This paid-up value is your new sum assured.
In such instances, if you choose to terminate your plan, the cash surrender value of the policy will be equal to the sum of the paid-up value and the bonuses accumulated multiplied by the surrender value factor of the policy. In a nutshell, your special surrender value will be sizable if you’ve paid premiums for many years before terminating the plan.
Alternatives to Surrendering Your Life Insurance Plan
While knowing the cash surrender value of your policy is essential, surrendering it may not always be the most prudent course of action when in need of cash. Instead, you can look into selling your policy through life settlements.
If you qualify for such life settlements, chances are that you’ll receive a payout that’s much more sizable than the cash surrender value of your policy. This process essentially entails you selling the death benefits associated with the policy to a third-party buyer in exchange for a lump-sum payment. This exempts you from paying annual premiums and upon your death, the benefits of your life insurance plan are credited to the buyer.
Alternatively, if you have an endowment plan or unit-linked policy, you can also borrow against the said policy after you’ve paid premiums for at least 3 years. While the rules of borrowing may be different for different insurance providers, generally you can borrow up to 90% of your policy’s cash value. This will help you meet your immediate cash requirements without losing out on the policy’s coverage and death benefits.
Wrapping it Up
If you’re unable to pay your life insurance premiums or require immediate cash assistance, surrendering the policy may be a good choice. You can calculate the cash surrender value of your policy within seconds with the help of an online life insurance payout calculator. However, by surrendering your policy, you do stand to lose out on sizable benefits since the amount you receive is much lower than the premiums you’ve already paid. Thus, in such cases, looking into alternatives like life settlements or borrowing against the policy can be more prudent options than simple terminations.
https://www.policybazaar.com/life-insurance/general-info/articles/understanding-surrender-value-in-insurance/
full_pdf_text:
How to Calculate the Cash Surrender Value of Life Insurance
Keywords: cash surrender value, calculate surrender value, surrender value
Meta Description: Surrendering your life insurance plan terminates your coverage immediately. If you’re planning on surrendering your policy, knowing how to calculate its surrender value is important. Read on to find out more about cash surrender values and alternatives to surrendering your life insurance policy.
A life insurance policy can help you secure the financial future of your family members, even if anything unfortunate were to happen to you. However, there may be certain situations where you may need to surrender the policy before it matures. This is where knowing about the surrender value of your life insurance plan becomes essential.
Surrender Value and Associated Charges
Whether you’re tired of paying high premiums or you no longer have beneficiaries who need the death benefit from your plan, you need to know the cash surrender value of your life insurance policy before terminating it.
Cash surrender value refers to the amount you will receive from your insurer when you cancel a plan before it matures. In other words, if you terminate your policy mid-term, you may be eligible for a certain payout amount against the premiums you’ve paid. This depends up on the plan you have opted for and its underlying terms and conditions. Additionally, as per IRDAI (Unit Linked Insurance Products) Regulations, 2019, in case of a ULIP if you choose to terminate the policy after 5 years, no surrender charges can be imposed on it by your insurance provider. However in case of other products, charges will be applicable.
If you’re wondering how this surrender value is calculated, fret not, for we’ve got you covered! In this article, we review the process of calculating the surrender value of a life insurance plan as well as the alternatives to surrendering your policy.
How is Cash Surrender Value Calculated?
Calculating the surrender value of your insurance plan is quite simple. All you need to do is add the total premiums paid and subtract the charges levied by your insurer for surrendering the plan mid-term.
However, this is not a blanket calculation process. Before you can calculate the surrender value of your life insurance policy, you must know the difference between guaranteed surrender value and special surrender value as the cash surrender value of your policy will be hinged on these two key concepts when it comes to traditional life insurance plans. For ULIPs, a discontinuance charge will apply between the first to fifth years.
Surrender Values and How to Calculate Them
Guaranteed Surrender Value: This is the amount that is guaranteed to the policyholder in case there is a voluntary termination of the policy by the policyholder prior to the maturity of the policy. You can claim this surrender value after 3 years of paying your insurance premiums.
Alternatives to Surrendering Your Life Insurance Plan
While knowing the cash surrender value of your policy is essential, surrendering it may not always be the most prudent course of action when in need of cash.
Alternatively, if you have an endowment plan, you can also borrow against the said policy after you’ve paid premiums for at least 3 years. For ULIPs, they do not have a loan option but they do offer a partial withdrawal option for policyholders. While the rules of borrowing may be different for different insurance providers, generally you may borrow up to 90% of your policy’s cash value. This may help you meet your immediate cash requirements without losing out on the policy’s coverage and death benefits.
Wrapping it up
You can calculate the cash surrender value of your policy within seconds with the help of an online life insurance payout calculator. However, by surrendering your policy, you do stand to lose out on sizable benefits since the amount you receive will be lower than the premiums you’ve already paid. Thus, in such cases, looking into alternatives like loans against policy depending on the rules of borrowing of the insurers or partial withdrawals in case of ULIPs may be more prudent options than simple terminations.
Disclaimer:
In this policy, the investment risk in investment portfolio is borne by the policyholder. Investment in ULIPs is subject to risks associated with the capital markets. The policy holder is solely responsible for his/her decisions while investing in ULIPs. The views stated in this article is not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions.
